blocks.text: Chapter 4.
Caching: Forgetting as a Feature
The ideas in 30 seconds
Every memory system faces the same trade-off — big and slow versus small and fast — and the solution everywhere is a cache: a small, fast store holding the items you are most likely to need next.

The optimal eviction rule, Bélády's algorithm, requires clairvoyance; the best practical substitute is Least Recently Used (LRU), which works because the past is a strong predictor of the near future.
Caching is about space as much as speed: Amazon's warehouses and content delivery networks work by moving copies of things physically closer to the people who will want them.
The Noguchi filing system — always return a used file to the front — is a provably near-optimal self-organizing cache, which means an untidy pile ordered by recent use is efficient, not lazy.

John Anderson's research shows human forgetting tracks the statistics of the real world: the probability we need a memory decays over time in exactly the pattern our recall follows, so memory is tuned to reality rather than failing.

Slower recall with age is largely a search problem, not decay — a fuller archive simply takes longer to search, and some "forgetting" is the necessary price of keeping the right things instantly available.
The Memory Hierarchy: Small, Fast, and Close

Computers face a brutal engineering trade-off: memory can be fast or plentiful, but not both at once, at any reasonable price. The answer, sketched as early as 1946 by Arthur Burks, Herman Goldstine, and John von Neumann, is a memory hierarchy — a pyramid of storage layers, each level smaller and faster than the one below it.

The key innovation came in 1962, when computing pioneer Maurice Wilkes proposed what he called a "slave memory": a small, quick store that automatically keeps copies of the items most recently pulled from the big, slow store. Since whatever you just needed is what you're most likely to need again, most requests can be served from the fast layer. IBM built the idea into the System/360 Model 85 in 1969, and its engineers gave it the name that stuck: cache.

The gamble pays off because of locality of reference — programs (and people) don't touch their data uniformly. They return to the same items again and again in bursts. A request the cache can serve is a cache hit; one that forces a trip to slower storage is a cache miss. With good locality, a cache holding perhaps one percent of the data can serve the vast majority of requests at full speed.

The stakes have only grown. Processors have improved far faster than memory has, opening a gap engineers call the memory wall: a modern CPU can execute hundreds of instructions in the time it takes to fetch one item from main memory. Modern machines therefore stack multiple cache levels between processor and RAM, and the hierarchy continues outward — RAM caches the disk, the disk caches the network, and data centers cache the wider internet.

The same architecture describes your life. Your desk caches your filing cabinet; your closet caches your basement; your bag caches your home. The design question is never "how do I store everything?" but "what belongs in the small, fast layer right now?" — which makes the real problem deciding what to remove.
Eviction: Bélády's Clairvoyant Ideal and the Power of LRU

A cache is by definition too small to hold everything, so its defining problem is cache eviction: when a new item arrives and the cache is full, what do you throw out? In 1966, IBM researcher László Bélády — who had fled Hungary in 1956 carrying almost nothing, a man with early practice deciding what to keep — published the definitive analysis, which became for over a decade the most cited paper in computer science.

Bélády's algorithm gives the theoretical optimum: evict the item you will need again furthest in the future. It is provably unbeatable and completely unimplementable, since it requires clairvoyance. Its value is as a benchmark — the standard against which every realizable policy is judged.

The realistic contenders are simple. Random eviction discards an arbitrary item; it performs surprisingly respectably, since important items that get evicted are soon re-cached. FIFO (first-in, first-out) evicts whatever has been in the cache longest, regardless of use. And LRU — Least Recently Used — evicts the item that has gone untouched for the longest time.

In both theory and practice, LRU comes closest to Bélády's clairvoyant ideal, and it usually wins by a large margin over FIFO. The reason is temporal locality: if you've used something recently, you'll probably use it again soon. LRU quietly inverts a common intuition. We assume the thing we haven't needed in ages is about to become relevant; the data say the opposite. The best predictor of the near future is the recent past, and the longer something has sat unused, the longer it is likely to keep sitting.

LRU also implies the best rule for when caches get layered or shared: never hold what you can't rank. If you must guess what to keep, keep what was touched last. That single heuristic — recency as the proxy for the unknowable future — is the chapter's workhorse, and it powers everything from your browser's back button and page cache to the filing system below.
Caching in Space: Warehouses, CDNs, and the Geography of Speed
Bélády's problem is about time, but caching's other dimension is distance: latency is geography. If you cannot make data travel faster, move it closer before it's requested.

That is the entire business model of the content delivery network (CDN). Akamai, the pioneering CDN spun out of MIT, places servers inside internet providers around the world and fills them with copies of popular content, so a video or webpage loads from a machine a few miles away instead of a data center on another continent. Netflix does the same, pre-loading its most-watched titles onto hard drives embedded deep inside ISPs — most of what the world streams is served from a cache down the street.

Amazon applies identical logic to physical goods. Its fulfillment warehouses act as regional caches, stocked according to what nearby customers are statistically likely to order. The company even patented "anticipatory shipping" in 2012 — moving items toward a region, or into trucks, before anyone has bought them, on the strength of predicted demand. Notably, many Amazon warehouses skip tidy categorical organization for chaotic storage: items shelved wherever space exists, tracked by computer. As with the messy desk, external order matters less than retrieval speed.

The domestic translation is direct. Storage advice usually obsesses over categories — all batteries together, all tools together. Caching says the primary sort key should be frequency and recency of use, and the primary design variable should be distance from the point of use. Keep the everyday items at hand, the monthly items in the closet, the yearly items in the basement or storage unit — a full memory hierarchy in drywall. The book's authors note you can even run a deliberate cache within a room: a box or valet stand near the door holding exactly the things about to leave the house with you.

One caution from the multi-level hierarchy: each layer should hold what earns its place there. Filling premium space — the kitchen counter, your entryway, your attention — with rarely used items is the household version of a badly managed cache: constant misses, endless trips to slow storage.
Noguchi's Filing System: The Mess That Is Provably Optimal

Japanese economist Yukio Noguchi, drowning in documents, devised what he called the "super" filing system. Put each document in an envelope, and stand all envelopes in one box. New envelopes go in at the left end. Whenever you use a file, don't return it to its "proper" place — put it back at the left end too. Search always starts from the left.

The result: the box self-organizes. Frequently and recently used files hover near the left, where searches find them almost instantly; dormant files drift steadily rightward, sorting themselves into deep storage — and nominating themselves for eventual disposal — without any effort from you. Noguchi arrived at this as a pragmatic hack, but computer scientists know the move-to-front rule as a self-organizing list, and in 1985 Daniel Sleator and Robert Tarjan proved a remarkable result: against any sequence of requests, move-to-front's total search cost stays within a constant factor of the best possible arrangement chosen by a clairvoyant adversary. Noguchi's box is, in a precise sense, as good as filing gets without foreknowledge — because it is LRU.

Now tip the box on its back and you have the stack of papers on your desk. A pile you always pull from and return to the top is a move-to-front list: the most relevant documents float up, the irrelevant sink. Far from a guilty failure of discipline, the pile is a well-engineered cache, and "organizing" it alphabetically would likely make retrieval slower.

The same logic settles a wardrobe debate. Organizing gurus offer many purge criteria — Martha Stewart's include how long you've owned an item, whether it still functions, whether it's a duplicate, and when you last wore it. Cache theory says the last question dominates: recency of use is the best available forecast of future use. Hang clothes back at one end of the rod after wearing them, and the far end becomes an automatic, honest donation queue.
Human Memory: Forgetting Tuned to Reality

Carnegie Mellon psychologist John Anderson set out in the late 1980s to do a "rational analysis" of memory — asking not how memory fails, but what problem it is optimally solving. His conclusion: the mind is a cache, and forgetting is its eviction policy.

With Lael Schooler, Anderson measured the statistical structure of the environment memory must serve. Analyzing years of New York Times headlines (and, separately, parents' speech to children), they found that a word's probability of appearing again declines lawfully with the time since its last appearance — a power-law decay in which recent items are overwhelmingly likely to recur and long-absent ones almost never do. Then came the striking part: human forgetting curves, from Ebbinghaus's classic self-experiments onward, follow essentially the same function. The rate at which memories become harder to retrieve mirrors the rate at which the world stops asking for them. Memory isn't leaking; it is running LRU against an accurately estimated environment, keeping the likely-needed available and letting the unlikely-needed sink into the box's far end.

This reframes aging. Linguist Michael Ramscar's modeling showed that much of older adults' slower recall is predicted simply by database size: someone with decades more vocabulary, names, and facts is searching a vastly larger archive, so longer lookups signal accumulated knowledge, not decay. The occasional lag — the tip-of-the-tongue stall — is a cache miss while the request goes to deeper storage, not evidence the item is gone.

The practical corollaries are gentle. Expect retrieval speed to trade off against library size, and don't pathologize the trade. Exploit the design instead of fighting it: since availability tracks recency, refresh what you'll need — review notes before the meeting, rehearse names on the way to the party. And forgive your forgetting of what you haven't touched in years. By the environment's own statistics, you almost certainly weren't going to need it.
In practice
When you must clear space — closet, desk, phone, inbox — evict by LRU: whatever you've gone longest without touching goes first, regardless of sentiment or category.
Reorganize storage by distance-to-use: daily items within arm's reach, monthly items one step away, yearly items in deep storage, mirroring a memory hierarchy.
Run a Noguchi system for papers or files: one sequence, always return the item you just used to the front, and let the back end nominate itself for disposal.
Stop apologizing for the ordered mess: keep a working pile or "recent" folder where used items go on top, and resist alphabetizing what recency already sorts.
Hang worn clothes at one end of the rod; after six months, donate from the other end without deliberation.
Pre-warm your own cache: briefly review names, notes, or facts shortly before you'll need them, since recent access is what makes memories instantly retrievable.
